museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Discounting deepens, home computers look safer than consoles, and UK players find the year through magazines, cassettes and the high street rather than through a single national launch event.
museum.curatorNote: February has fewer clean game-release anchors than the summer months. This draft treats it as a market-context exhibit and marks release-window items clearly.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: A month of pressure rather than spectacle.

items.title: The home-computer price war bites harder
items.body: TI and Commodore continued turning price into a weapon, making computers seem newly reachable while damaging margins underneath the market.
items.sourceNote: TI timelines list February price moves and ongoing rebate pressure.

items.title: Console confidence keeps slipping in North America
items.body: Retailers had too much stock and too little certainty. In Britain, this was filtered through import prices, magazine warnings and a stronger local microcomputer story.
items.sourceNote: Video game crash summaries describe the market shrinking through 1983.

items.title: Arcade games remain the public benchmark
items.body: Even as home retail stuttered, the cabinet floor still supplied the images players wanted: racing, vector lines, characters and spectacle.

items.title: UK type-in culture stays productive
items.body: For many home-computer owners, the act of making a game from a magazine listing was still part of the appeal.

items.title: The Famicom future is not yet visible in Britain
items.body: Nintendo's 1983 Japanese console was still months away and not yet a UK retail object. February's British future looked more like Spectrum cassettes than gamepads.
items.sourceNote: Famicom launched in Japan in July 1983.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight games and shelf objects from the February 1983 atmosphere.

items.title: Popeye
items.body: Nintendo's cartoon cabinet remained part of early-1983 arcade rotation.
items.sourceNote: Popeye chart strength is documented in spring 1983.

items.title: Pole Position
items.body: Still the racing cabinet most likely to make other screens look slow.

items.title: Q*bert
items.body: A strange character hit that kept the arcade from feeling creatively exhausted.

items.title: Pitfall!
items.body: Still the strong argument for buying carefully rather than abandoning cartridges altogether.

items.title: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
items.body: A recent licensed cartridge increasingly discussed as a warning object.

items.title: The Hobbit
items.body: An adventure that kept British micro owners thinking about text, memory and strange parser behaviour.

items.title: Manic Miner build-up
items.body: Matthew Smith's platformer was not yet the chart object it would become, but the UK Spectrum scene that produced it was ready.
items.sourceNote: Manic Miner appears later in 1983; not a February release.

items.title: Home-computer type-in games
items.body: Magazine code listings filled gaps between commercial cassette purchases.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The machines are being repriced in public.

items.title: Commodore VIC-20 and C64
items.body: Commodore's pricing strategy made computers feel competitive with consoles as games machines.

items.title: TI-99/4A
items.body: The TI machine was selling but increasingly caught in a loss-making race.

items.title: Atari 2600
items.body: A huge installed base could not protect the machine from overloaded shelves and declining trust.

items.title: BBC Micro and ZX Spectrum
items.body: For UK homes, the serious-versus-cheap computer decision shaped gaming more than the US console fight did.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: February's exhibit is mostly paper evidence.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Console confidence under pressure
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for February 1983.
items.curatorNote: A US games magazine trying to cover a market whose confidence was already changing.
items.body: A US games magazine trying to cover a market whose confidence was already changing.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: Arcade glamour, bedroom machines
items.curatorNote: C&VG kept the UK reader's frame wide: cabinets, consoles, micros and listings together.
items.body: C&VG kept the UK reader's frame wide: cabinets, consoles, micros and listings together.

items.title: Your Computer
items.headline: Choosing a micro in a price-war year
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Your Computer cover for February 1983.
items.curatorNote: Buying advice doubled as games advice when the computer was becoming the games machine.
items.body: Buying advice doubled as games advice when the computer was becoming the games machine.

items.title: ZX Computing
items.headline: Spectrum culture gathers speed
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed ZX Computing cover for February 1983.
items.curatorNote: The Spectrum press made the machine feel communal before the hit-game flood fully arrived.
items.body: The Spectrum press made the machine feel communal before the hit-game flood fully arrived.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Information moved slowly, but it moved constantly.

items.title: Retail gossip mattered
items.body: Which machine was cheaper this week could be as important as which game reviewed well.

items.title: Magazine code made participation possible
items.body: A reader could turn print into play with enough patience.

items.title: School rooms were networks
items.body: Games travelled as names, tapes and rumours between classmates.

items.title: No central database existed
items.body: Release knowledge was local, late and often contradictory.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: February felt unsettled rather than empty.

items.title: The bargain looked tempting
items.body: Cheap cartridges and cheaper computers both asked for attention.

items.title: The micro felt safer
items.body: A computer could be defended as useful even when bought for games.

items.title: Arcades still had authority
items.body: The cabinet remained the version of the future no home machine quite matched.

items.title: Certainty was scarce
items.body: A magazine could make a machine look essential one week and questionable the next.
